ontology.foodex2_description: Samosa — fried pastry snack with filling; used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for samosa-based chaat dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for samosa dahi chaat. A0CKP (samosa) used as the closest defensible anchor. Yogurt and chutney toppings are not captured in this code.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of samosa dahi chaat in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising two samosas topped with yogurt, tamarind chutney, green chutney, chaat masala, and sev.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa Dhai Chat — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Samosa Dhai Chat (cr_id G00011067). All per-100g macros, micronutrients, and minerals are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous fried pastry snack data as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item.

gi_source.title: Samosa (fried potato-filled pastry) — analogous GI estimate based on potato-filled fried pastry; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for samosa dahi chaat in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: fried potato-filled pastry (samosa), which has a reported GI of approximately 60–70 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The yogurt topping provides a modest GI-lowering effect (dairy lowers postprandial glucose response), and the tamarind chutney adds concentrated sugars. A composite GI of 65 (medium) is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (65 × 33.16 × 2.0) / 100 = 43.1 / 2 ≈ 21.6 per 100 g serving equivalent; calculated as GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 65 × 66.32 / 100 = 43.1 for 200 g serving, reported here as GL per serving (200 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 388 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (166 mg/100 g) and additional sodium from chaat masala and tamarind chutney toppings
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit portion size to no more than 100 g and request minimal chaat masala and tamarind chutney, as these condiments substantially increase the already-elevated sodium load beyond the base 388 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load from refined wheat pastry and potato filling (33.2 g carbohydrate/100 g, GI ~65) combined with concentrated sugars in tamarind chutney
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit samosa dahi chaat due to its high glycaemic load; if consumed, limit to a single small samosa (~50 g), omit or minimise tamarind chutney, and pair with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 17.47 g/100 g including saturated fat ~3.8 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g from deep-frying, combined with high sodium 388 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of samosa dahi chaat due to its combination of deep-fried saturated and trans fats, high sodium, and refined carbohydrates, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles, blood pressure, and endothelial function; a baked samosa variant with reduced chutney is a preferable alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried base generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); potato and green chilli are nightshade vegetables; trans fat 0.2 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid samosa dahi chaat due to the pro-inflammatory combination of deep-frying AGEs, nightshade vegetables (potato, green chilli), and trans fat; request a baked samosa base and monitor for joint flare after consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Samosa Dahi Chaat (also spelled Samosa Dhai Chat) is a popular Indian-origin street food and hawker snack widely available in Singapore, particularly in Indian food courts and hawker centres. The dish consists of one or two deep-fried samosas — triangular pastries made from refined wheat flour (maida) filled with spiced mashed potato and green peas — that are broken open and topped with generous amounts of plain yogurt (dahi), sweet tamarind chutney, green coriander-mint chutney, chaat masala spice blend, and crispy sev (thin fried chickpea noodles). The result is a complex multi-textured dish combining the crispy, oily pastry shell with the soft potato filling, cool creamy yogurt, tangy-sweet tamarind, and the sharp heat of chaat masala. Nutritionally, samosa dahi chaat is energy-dense at approximately 310 kcal/100 g, with a significant fat content of 17.47 g/100 g from deep-frying and a high carbohydrate load of 33.16 g/100 g from the refined pastry and potato filling. Sodium is elevated at 388 mg/100 g, and the condiment toppings add further sodium beyond the base profile. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium-high), making the glycaemic load substantial for a typical 200 g serving. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control and condiment modification. The yogurt topping provides modest protein and calcium benefit and a minor GI-lowering effect, but is insufficient to offset the overall nutritional concerns of the dish. In Singapore, samosa dahi chaat is commonly consumed as a snack, appetiser, or light meal at lunch or dinner, and is a staple of Indian hawker cuisine.

What is dahi chaat made of?

ingredient_synonyms.chaat masala: Indian spice blend for chaat dishes
ingredient_synonyms.samosa: fried triangular pastry with spiced filling

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Samosa Dhai Chat (cr_id G00011067) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous fried pastry data. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All other fields are derived from these approved sources.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for samosa dahi chaat is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g of 0.2 g/100 g is carried forward from the input health_suitability clinical notes; this value is consistent with deep-fried refined pastry products and is documented in the rheumatoid arthritis caution in the source record. 3. saturated_fat_g of 3.8 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for samosa (fried, potato-filled pastry) as the closest approved-source analogue; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this dish, and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue with complete micronutrient data is available as of 2026. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for samosa dahi chaat exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 6. FoodOn URI mapped to snack food (chaat) as the closest defensible class; no exact dish-level URI exists. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (samosa) used as closest anchor; composite dish toppings not captured. 8. Chinese names are transliterations and may not reflect standard culinary Chinese terminology; native Chinese speakers may use descriptive terms rather than transliterations for this dish.
